channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: The World Cup has always been beset by scandal and strife
item.description: So has FIFA, the outfit that administers it

item.title: The World Cup is an exception. Fun is more fragmented than ever
item.description: A local-content boom is rewriting the rules of entertainmentГўВҖВ”and of soft power

item.title: Too much Chinese science is ignored by the West
item.description: A bad reputation and cultural ignorance are probably responsible

item.title: Donald TrumpГўВҖВҷs least bad option in Iran
item.description: He must swallow his pride and accept a deal worse than the pre-war status quo

item.title: AmericaГўВҖВҷs mayors join the scrabble to become influencers
item.description: A dispatch from Long Beach, California

item.title: Eat your electronics, boost your health
item.description: Our podcast on science and technology. This week, why swallowing devices could soon support your health

item.title: The Green Party responds to our leader arguing that the new leftГўВҖВҷs policies threaten prosperity
item.description: The green economy creates thousands of jobs and strengthens energy security, argues Sarah Wakefield

item.title: British politicians are racing to the hard right
item.description: Amid riots in Belfast, Restore promises to put ГўВҖВңmurderous third-world savages to deathГўВҖВқ

item.title: From morning in America to endless conflict
Fear of the SaaSpocalypse is tormenting techland
item.description: Software once ate the world. Now it is in danger of eating itself

item.title: President Prabowo Subianto responds to our recent briefing
item.description: We need to work for our common future and deliver on the platform on which we were elected, he says

item.title: The US in Brief: An oysterman catches Maine
item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: Number crunch: why Britons ignore immigrant drop
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: aboard a US nuclear sub and how to tackle wrinkles

item.title: AmericaГўВҖВҷs apocalyptic capitalism
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: An interview with South KoreaГўВҖВҷs president
item.description: Lee Jae Myung has put his country on track again, but challenges loom

item.title: Wall StreetГўВҖВҷs undignified SpaceX mania
item.description: Is that a rocket in your lobby, or are you just happy to see me?ГӮВ

item.title: Why Turkey likes NATO again
item.description: The alliance is in trouble, but Erdogan is enthusiastic

item.title: Ukrainian strikes are inflicting pain deep inside Russia
item.description: Our modelling suggests they are doing even more damage than commonly assumed

item.title: Why strongmen are wrong to loathe Europe
item.description: The leaders of America, China and Russia scorn consensual politics. That is a mistake

item.title: Blighty newsletter: Britain according to MAGA
item.description: For some Americans, the country has become a cautionary tale, writes Georgia Banjo, our Britain correspondent

item.title: What happens when a presidential vote is a dead heat?
item.description: Peru, looking for its ninth president in a decade, is in a tight spot

item.title: The Federal Reserve must soon give Donald Trump bad news
item.description: Kevin Warsh, the unlucky new chairman, has seen his case for lower interest rates disintegrate

item.title: Inside EthiopiaГўВҖВҷs Chinese megafactory
item.description: Our weekly podcast on China. This week, can Chinese investors help Ethiopia become a factory of the world?

item.title: AppleГўВҖВҷs new Siri is a dark horse in the AI race
item.description: The iPhone-maker does not need to build models to cash in on the technology

item.title: Saint or sinner: Antoni GaudГғВӯГўВҖВҷs polarising style
item.description: A hundred years after his death, the Spanish architect is both loved and reviled

item.title: BritainГўВҖВҷs privatised utilities are a mess
item.description: But public ownership is a red herring

item.description: Also on the daily podcast: Meet CongoГўВҖВҷs rebels and Indian restaurants in Japan

item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: ArmeniaГўВҖВҷs election is a setback for Vladimir Putin
item.description: RussiaГўВҖВҷs dirty tricks are failing to stop its pivot to the West

item.title: China is innovative. Its economy is a mess. Which will win out?
item.description: A question that will define the 21st century

item.title: A bidding war erupts for the worldГўВҖВҷs oldest bank
item.description: Monte dei Paschi di Siena is suddenly all the rage

item.title: How Israel is frustrating Donald TrumpГўВҖВҷs Iran plans
item.description: Binyamin Netanyahu has defied America twice in recent days

item.title: The ageing protesters trying to topple WashingtonГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВңego archГўВҖВқ
item.description: Donald Trump has energised his fellow boomers in opposition

item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, examines why conflict exerts a ГўВҖВңstrange pullГўВҖВқ

item.description: An opportunity to write for The Economist

item.title: Will artificial intelligence soon escape human control?

item.description: So nukes are off the agenda as Xi Jinping heads to Pyongyang

item.title: The World Cup will test MexicoГўВҖВҷs control over its territory
item.description: Donald Trump, who says the government is too weak to combat the drug gangs, will be watching

item.title: Money troubles are driving IndiaГўВҖВҷs states to drink
item.description: Things are so dire that one state has produced a rational alcohol policy

item.title: Against Russia, Europe needs a peace-through-strength strategy
item.description: Talk of an envoy for negotiations is premature, writes Anders Fogh Rasmussen

item.title: Robots could soon be delivering your pizza
item.description: An Estonian startup says its machines are now cheaper than human couriers

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: A modest proposal on Cuba
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, on why the island should become AmericaГўВҖВҷs 51st state

item.title: How to Retire at 30
item.description: Inside the FIRE (Financial Independence, Retire Early) movement

item.title: How hot is AmericaГўВҖВҷs labour market?ГӮВ
item.description: At the moment, balmy. But it wouldnГўВҖВҷt take a lot to require some Fed air-conditioning

item.title: Should priests have to report child abuse disclosed in confession?
item.description: American states take different approaches

item.title: Warning signs from two rival fighter-jet projects
item.description: An effort to build a plane between Britain, Italy and Japan risks similar problems to one by France, Germany and Spain

item.description: Vitamins, applied properly, can partially reverse the effects of ageing

item.description: Our list spans horror, comedy and documentary

item.title: How many times a day do you think about Alexander the Great?
item.description: A new book is as riveting as its protagonist was

item.description: Startups are combining AI and genetics to make more food for less money

item.description: As its industries of the future take off, old ones still flout basic safety procedures
